interpretations.title: Results and Interpretations
rows: Eye sockets, muscles, nerves, and bones show no evidence of injury, swelling, or structural abnormality
No serious disease present; further investigation not required
Visible crack or break in the orbital bone, may involve one or more walls
Confirms traumatic injury; guides surgical repair and follow-up imaging
Optic nerve appears thickened or abnormally enhanced on images
Suggests possible optic neuritis or compressive lesion; often requires MRI for further evaluation
A distinct, fluid-filled mass is identified. After contrast injection, the outer border (rim) of the mass becomes significantly brighter.
This appearance is highly suggestive of an orbital abscess (a collection of pus). This is a serious infection that often requires urgent surgical drainage and antibiotics.

faqs.question: Is CT ORBIT safe for kids?
faqs.answer: Yes. Paediatric protocols cut radiation by up to 70 %. Guardians may stay in the room behind lead shielding.

faqs.question: Do I need an appointment?
faqs.answer: Walk-ins accepted for trauma; appointments preferred for contrast studies. Book online 24/7 at cadabamsdiagnostics.com.

faqs.question: How much does a CT ORBIT cost?
faqs.answer: Transparent, all-inclusive pricing starts at ₹3,500 (non-contrast) and ₹5,200 (contrast). Insurance cashless and EMI options available.

faqs.question: Is the radiation from a CT scan dangerous?
faqs.answer: Cadabams Diagnostics is committed to patient safety and uses modern, state-of-the-art CT equipment with low-dose protocols. The amount of radiation used for a **CT Orbit Scan** is minimal and carefully controlled. For any medically necessary scan, the diagnostic benefit of obtaining a fast, accurate diagnosis is considered far greater than the small potential risk associated with the radiation exposure.

faqs.question: When will I get my results?
faqs.answer: The radiologist's comprehensive report is typically finalized and sent to your referring doctor within 24-48 hours of your scan. Your doctor will then schedule a follow-up appointment with you to discuss the findings and what they mean for your health and any next steps.

seo.title: CT Orbits Test at Cadabams Diagnostics | Fast & Precise Orbit Scan
seo.description: Get a quick, low-radiation CT ORBIT scan at Cadabams Diagnostics. Explore uses, prep tips, risks, and results in easy-to-understand language.

test.markdown:
## CT Orbit Scan Overview

A \*\*CT ORBIT\*\* (computed tomography of the eye orbits) is a painless, 10-minute imaging study that creates high-resolution, 3-D pictures of the eyes, eye muscles, optic nerves, and surrounding bone. At Cadabams Diagnostics, we use the latest 128-slice CT scanner to deliver sharp images with the lowest possible radiation dose, helping doctors spot problems early and plan treatment with confidence.
---
## What is CT Orbits Test?
- \*\*Definition\*\*: Specialised CT scan focused on the eye sockets (orbits).
- \*\*Technology\*\*: X-ray beams rotate 360° around the head; a computer reconstructs cross-sectional slices.
- \*\*Output\*\*: Detailed axial, coronal, and sagittal views that reveal soft tissue, bone, and blood vessels in under 10 minutes.
---
## When and Who Needs to Take a CT Orbits Test?
### Common Scenarios
- Trauma after an eye injury or facial fracture
- Persistent double vision or bulging eyes
- Suspected orbital infection, tumour, or thyroid eye disease
- Pre-surgical planning for cataract, squint, or orbital decompression
- Evaluation of unexplained vision loss or optic nerve swelling
---

## Risks & Limitations of the CT Orbit Scan

| Risk | How Cadabams Mitigates |
|---|---|
| Radiation exposure | Ultra-low-dose protocols; average dose <1 mSv |
| Allergy to contrast | Pre-test screening, on-site resuscitation kit |
| Claustrophobia | Wide-bore scanner; calming music & staff support |
\*\*Limitation\*\*: CT is less detailed than MRI for subtle nerve or brain tissue; your clinician will advise if MRI is required.
---
## Types of CT Orbits Test
1. \*\*Non-Contrast CT Orbits\*\* – quick screening for fractures or foreign bodies
2. \*\*Contrast-Enhanced CT Orbits\*\* – iodine-based dye highlights blood vessels and tumours
3. \*\*CT Orbit Angiography\*\* – 3-D vessel mapping before surgery or embolisation

## List of Parameters Considered During the CT Orbit Scan

- Orbital wall thickness & integrity
- Extra-ocular muscle size & symmetry
- Optic nerve diameter & enhancement pattern
- Globe position (proptosis index)
- Presence of calcification, haemorrhage, or foreign body
---

## Reasons for Taking a CT Orbit Scan

- Rule out orbital cellulitis or abscess
- Detect intra-ocular or orbital tumours (retinoblastoma, haemangioma)
- Assess thyroid eye disease activity
- Locate metallic or glass foreign bodies after accidents
- Measure orbital volume before reconstructive surgery

## Benefits of Taking the Test

- \*\*Speed\*\*: Scan completed in 5–10 minutes
- \*\*Accuracy\*\*: Sub-millimetre resolution detects 0.5 mm lesions
- \*\*Comfort\*\*: Open gantry; no loud noises
- \*\*Guidance\*\*: Images uploaded instantly to Cadabams secure cloud for same-day radiologist reporting

## Illnesses Diagnosed with CT Orbit Scan

- Orbital fractures (blow-out, tripod)
- Graves’ ophthalmopathy
- Optic neuritis
- Cavernous haemangioma
- Retinoblastoma (paediatric)
- Orbital cellulitis or mucormycosis
- Foreign body granuloma

## Preparing for a CT Orbit Scan

- \*\*Fasting\*\*: Not required for non-contrast scan; 4-hour fast for contrast study
- \*\*Clothing\*\*: Wear metal-free attire or change into gown
- \*\*Medications\*\*: Continue regular medicines unless told otherwise
- \*\*Jewellery\*\*: Remove earrings, glasses, and piercings near the face
---
## Pre-requisites
- Doctor’s prescription or online request via Cadabams portal
- Recent kidney function test (for contrast studies)
- Pregnancy test if applicable (CT avoided unless emergency)
---
## Best Time to Take the CT Orbits Test
- \*\*Acute trauma\*\*: Within 24 hours of injury
- \*\*Thyroid eye disease\*\*: At diagnosis and every 6–12 months for monitoring
- \*\*Pre-surgery\*\*: 1–2 weeks before planned operation
---
## Eligibility
- \*\*Adults & Children\*\*: Safe for all ages; paediatric parameters reduce dose
- \*\*Weight limit\*\*: Up to 200 kg (scanner table capacity)
- \*\*Pregnancy\*\*: Deferred unless life-threatening emergency; shielding used if unavoidable
---
## Procedure for Taking a CT Orbits Test
1. \*\*Check-in & Consent\*\* (5 min)
2. \*\*Screening Questionnaire\*\* (allergies, kidney health)
3. \*\*Positioning\*\* – lie on your back, head on cushioned holder
4. \*\*Scan\*\* – table slides into gantry; keep still for 2–3 minutes
5. \*\*Contrast injection\*\* (if ordered) – quick IV push via auto-injector
6. \*\*Observation\*\* – 15-minute post-contrast monitoring for rare reactions
---
## Caution Before Taking the Test
- Inform staff of asthma, heart disease, or previous severe allergies
- Remove hearing aids, removable dental work
- Avoid caffeine if prone to claustrophobia; meditation apps available in waiting lounge
